title: Running .sif container with podman has path error?
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
I'm trying to run a Singularity .sif container with podman as described here: 
https://www.redhat.com/en/blog/expanding-podman-capabilities-deploy-sif-formatted-containers 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  Downloaded .sif file from https://cloud.sylabs.io/library/godlovedc/funny/lolcow

2.  Run the .sif with $ podman run -t sif:godlovedc_funny_lolcow.sif

3.

**Describe the results you received:**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/184965528-566e9ad0-7020-41d8-a003-5aa5a9dbcf68.png)


**Describe the results you expected:**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/184965734-b06bef59-60b6-4042-b305-9ba28a430347.png)



**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
podman version 4.1.1
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

Yes

Running podman in RHEL 9 virtual machine

comments.body: does it work if you install `fakeroot` manually (`yum install -y fakeroot`)?

I installed fakeroot thanks, and I'm now getting this: Probably a network problem and not podman but...
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/185207708-5a464673-cd0b-4dff-ad73-e62c6641141d.png)

Okay it looks like it is working thanks for the help. So run it without the -t flag then?
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/185227770-01ad8521-41d5-4c01-a203-94ffe22fd1d2.png)

I don't think the `-t` flag affects how the image is extracted, it will only affect how the container runs once the image is already in the local storage.

The error you've seen seems to come from fakeroot itself, probably a race condition where the target process already exited.
Okay that makes sense, thank you. And running a sif with podman is only possible with podman version >= 4.0 right?
yes, correct

I have a container .sif that I'm running with podman, but it seems the environment variables which I set in the original Singularity definition file are not maintained in the final podman container. Below the white terminal shows the Singularity environment variables. Is this expected behavior for the environment variables to not be maintained?
![podman error](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/185448308-537a270a-d309-4c6c-b861-3ef9bbedb7e4.PNG)


![podman error 2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/185448584-0023a011-00f1-4c24-b51a-fe62ba2c3182.PNG)
@mtrmac PTAL

The `%environment` section is supposed to be interpreted. I wouldn’t be too surprised if there were corner cases we were not handling correctly, or possibly even if the implementation were looking for data in a completely incorrect place.

See if the resulting Podman container contains `/podman/runscript`, and if the contents are suggestive.

Failing that, we’d need a complete step-by-step reproducer.

Here is the /podman/runscript. It doesn't seem suggestive, all of the variables seem correct but for some reason they aren't set in the container?
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/42785514/185671881-11f2db64-f6ec-4d1d-a73c-882533a91be4.png)

Ah, sure. If you override the command to run `/bin/bash` instead of `/podman/runscript`, the environment values in `/podman/runscript` can’t be applied.

The official `%environment` documentation more or less suggests that arbitrary shell syntax is supposed to be accepted (and the above, with the `export` command, is an example), so we can’t convert the `%environment` section into OCI environment settings (which would show up when running `/bin/bash` directly).

Okay thanks, but I'm still not following. So I can't use /bin/bash as a shell for the podman run command at all then? And I can't use export in the Singularity since it is too bash specific? How would I adjust that script to conform to /bin/sh so it all just works? Thanks for any help.

> Ah, sure. If you override the command to run `/bin/bash` instead of `/podman/runscript`, the environment values in `/podman/runscript` can’t be applied.
> 
> The official `%environment` documentation more or less suggests that arbitrary shell syntax is supposed to be accepted (and the above, with the `export` command, is an example), so we can’t convert the `%environment` section into OCI environment settings (which would show up when running `/bin/bash` directly).

could we set `/podman/runscript` as the entrypoint that will execute the specified command afterwards?  The entrypoint could still be overridden but it is more rare

> Okay thanks, but I'm still not following.

I think at this point we are at the stage of documenting what happens and what the options are, and only starting to figure out how it can work better.

---

> could we set `/podman/runscript` as the entrypoint that will execute the specified command afterwards?

That looks attractive at a first glance.

Looking at this a bit more, Singularity actually packs _so many_ ways to start an image:
- `singularity instance start`: `/.singularity.d/actions/start`
- `singularity exec`: `/.singularity.d/actions/exec`
- `singularity shell`: `/.singularity.d/actions/shell`
- `singularity run`: `/.singularity.d/actions/run`
- `singularity test`: `/.singularity.d/actions/test`

(The `/.singularity` paths are hard-coded in the command that starts the container, so that seems to be the stable ABI point. In turn, the contents tend to be generated shell scripts. OTOH per https://docs.sylabs.io/guides/3.7/user-guide/environment_and_metadata.html#singularity-d-directory the format seems undocumented and not guaranteed to be stable.)

All of the above …/actions/… scripts, notably including the `shell` one, seem to be generated to include the configured `%environment`.

---

Now… what to do in the OCI context?

A lazy answer is to say “here’s your container, use OCI layers to edit things as you like” – e.g. now that the data is in an OCI container, add a layer that completely changes the startup mechanism, and abandon the` /.singularity` data. That’s only viable if the user has easily available the data / configuration to re-create the environment, which is not at all certain.

Another option is to recommend using the Singularity-generated scripts directly, e.g. `podman run $image /.singularity.d/actions/run/shell` instead of `podman run $image /bin/bash`. An advantage is that this seems likely to work, and it’s the alternative that’s most likely to replicate the original environment; a disadvantage is that this leads users to hard-code Singularity knowledge into the workflow, making it _harder_ to move to native OCI containers again.

Generating some `/podman/setup-environment-and-run-command` as an entry point would allow things like `podman run $image /bin/bash` to work natively. A downside is that this doesn’t allow reaching any of the other `…/actions/…` entry points.  The user could, of course, `podman run $image /.singularity/actions/test`, but that would run the `%environment` commands _twice_ — and because that’s more or less unconstrained shell, that might lead to unexpected results.

Alternatively, we could recommend `podman run $image {/.singularity.d/runscript,test}` (not running the top-level entry points, but other scripts started from the entrypoints) and the like, not running `%environment` twice but hard-coding _much more_ about how (some version of) Singularity constructs the set of shell scripts. That doesn’t look all that attractive either.

(We can also add some syntactic sugar instead of telling users to hard-code `/.singularity`, for some convenience, but that still wouldn’t remove the dependency on the specifics of the generated shell scripts, nor the whole point of users designing Singularity-specific Podman workflows.)

---

So… what to do here?

Given the overall goal of allowing Singularity-originated containers to be used like any other OCI containers, it seems attractive to me to not worry about the `exec`/`shell`/`test`/`instance start` entrypoints too much; let’s migrate `run`, and let users manually deal with the others (either by starting `/.singularity/*` manually, or preferably by moving the actual actions into an application-specific `/my-instance-start` shell script, and invoking that script from the Singularity definition file or directly via `podman run`.

Assuming the above, we would be only worrying about `podman run` with the primary run script, and `podman {run,exec} $shell_or_other_custom_command` — then it does seem quite attractive to make the OCI entry point something that interprets the environment.

What do others think?

Cc: @yhcote

> > Okay thanks, but I'm still not following.
> 
> I think at this point we are at the stage of documenting what happens and what the options are, and only starting to figure out how it can work better.
> 
> > could we set `/podman/runscript` as the entrypoint that will execute the specified command afterwards?
>

> * `singularity exec`: `/.singularity.d/actions/exec`
> * `singularity shell`: `/.singularity.d/actions/shell`
> * `singularity run`: `/.singularity.d/actions/run`
> * `singularity test`: `/.singularity.d/actions/test`
> 
> (The `/.singularity` paths are hard-coded in the command that starts the container, so that seems to be the stable ABI point. In turn, the contents tend to be generated shell scripts. OTOH per https://docs.sylabs.io/guides/3.7/user-guide/environment_and_metadata.html#singularity-d-directory the format seems undocumented and not guaranteed to be stable.)
>

That’s only viable if the user has easily available the data / configuration to re-create the environment, which is not at all certain.

An advantage is that this seems likely to work, and it’s the alternative that’s most likely to replicate the original environment; a disadvantage is that this leads users to hard-code Singularity knowledge into the workflow, making it _harder_ to move to native OCI containers again.

A downside is that this doesn’t allow reaching any of the other `…/actions/… entry points. The user could, of course, `podman run $image /.singularity/actions/test`, but that would run the `%environment` commands _twice_ — and because that’s more or less unconstrained shell, that might lead to unexpected results.

That doesn’t look all that attractive either.
> 
> (We can also add some syntactic sugar instead of telling users to hard-code `/.singularity`, for some convenience, but that still wouldn’t remove the dependency on the specifics of the generated shell scripts, nor the whole point of users designing Singularity-specific Podman workflows.)
>

> 
> Given the overall goal of allowing Singularity-originated containers to be used like any other OCI containers, it seems attractive to me to not worry about the `exec`/`shell`/`test`/`instance start` entrypoints too much; let’s migrate `run`, and let users manually deal with the others (either by starting `/.singularity/*` manually, or preferably by moving the actual actions into an application-specific `/my-instance-start` shell script, and invoking that script from the Singularity definition file or directly via `podman run`.
> 
> Assuming the above, we would be only worrying about `podman run` with the primary run script, and `podman {run,exec} $shell_or_other_custom_command` — then it does seem quite attractive to make the OCI entry point something that interprets the environment.
> 
> What do others think?
> 
> Cc: @yhcote

@mtrmac I think it's reasonable. The first pass was to mainly get each rudimentary pieces of running a SIF container in and develop from there. The support for launch scripts for the `SIF -> Podman` port is somewhat like dealing with a reversed funnel. Best is to concentrate on 80% of the use cases first and let the users guide what is useful to them.

In other words, starting with migrating `run` is a good first exercise.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Any update on this issue?
OK, @mtrmac: I'll find some time to work on this. Will sync with you and other devs to validate Podman internal stuff.

I am trying to run this same SIF on macOS-10.15.7. podman is built with homebrew. 

Does podman need to be specifically configured/built to support the SIF transport?

SIF is only supported for podman without --remote.  On a MAC or Windows, you are running podman --remote talking to Podman service running within a VM. (or WSL2).

You could enter the VM, via podman machine ssh, and then run podman with the sif file.
@yhcote friendly ping.  I think we lost this issue on the radar.
